concerns.text: FR-007 and SC-006 mandate a correlation coefficient ≥ 0.7 between SO tag trends and external metrics (GitHub stars/NPM) to 'validate' adoption. This creates a circular/tautological validation risk if the external metric is definitionally coupled to the same underlying phenomenon (e.g., GitHub stars often correlate with SO activity by definition). More critically, setting a hard threshold (r ≥ 0.7) as a success criterion for *validation* is scientifically unsound: a lower correlation (e.g., r=0.4) is a valid empirical finding indicating the proxy is noisy, not a 'failure' of the system. The spec treats a continuous empirical relationship as a binary pass/fail gate, which invalidates the scientific method for this claim.

The spec applies the Mann-Kendall test to monthly aggregated data (n≈108) without addressing serial correlation (autocorrelation). In time series of technology adoption, monthly counts are highly autocorrelated. The standard Mann-Kendall test assumes independent observations; applying it to autocorrelated data inflates Type I error rates (false positives for trends). The spec fails to mandate a pre-whitening procedure or a modified Mann-Kendall test (e.g., Hamed & Rao), rendering the 'statistically significant' claims in US-1 and FR-003 methodologically invalid.

FR-005 and SC-004 require Pearson correlation for co-occurrence clustering of tag pairs. Tag co-occurrence is a binary/count relationship (tags on the same post), not a continuous linear variable. Pearson correlation assumes linearity and normality, which are violated by sparse, binary co-occurrence matrices. This can produce spurious clusters. The spec should require Jaccard similarity or Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI) for this data type, or explicitly justify why Pearson is robust enough for this specific sparse distribution.

FR-004 and FR-009 mandate STL decomposition on monthly data (n=108) with a fixed seasonal period (12). STL requires a stable seasonal component. Many technology tags (especially emerging/declining ones) do not exhibit stable 12-month seasonality; forcing STL on non-seasonal series creates artificial seasonal artifacts. The spec must require a pre-test for seasonality (e.g., spectral analysis or Ljung-Box on residuals of a non-seasonal model) before applying STL, or allow alternative decomposition (e.g., Hodrick-Prescott) for non-seasonal series.

SC-004 requires validating clusters by comparing intra-cluster correlation to inter-cluster correlation via a 'two-sample t-test'. This assumes the correlation coefficients are independent samples, which they are not (they share tags). A permutation test or a bootstrap-based significance test for cluster coherence is required to account for the dependence structure of the correlation matrix.
